query: cost-effectiveness of specialty pharmaceuticals in treating inflammatory diseases

retrieved_docs.title: Biosimilars Use in Medicine for Inflammatory Diseases -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: For the cost-saving potential and improved patient access to agents for treating inflammatory diseases, recognition of biosimilars as safe and efficacious agents by patients, primary care clinicians, specialists, and other healthcare professionals is warranted to achieve market share. [23] The recognition of biosimilars as safe alternative agents to biologics may be subdued by education on the meticulous approval criteria required of biosimilars imposed by the FDA for prescribing clinicians and healthcare professionals. [4] [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Biosimilars Use in Medicine for Inflammatory Diseases -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Biosimilars ultimately demonstrate similarity to original biologics based on their characteristics of safety, efficacy, potency, tolerability but do not share similar cost considerations. Cost and prescribing considerations can ultimately make biosimilars the choice of biotherapeutics when prescribing biologics for inflammatory diseases. [24] [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Biosimilars Use in Medicine for Inflammatory Diseases -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Barriers that need to be considered when prescribing biosimilars are patients not responding to the switch in medications or receiving no cost benefits due to their managed care plans. [23] The cost and prescribing considerations are a predominant focus of biosimilars in the US, as decreasing costs will improve patient access to biotherapeutics. [24] [22] Cost variability for biosimilars may also affect their presence and use on the US market. Competition from other biopharmaceuticals such as price drops, incentives, and reimbursements from existing biologics may narrow the price gap between original biologics and biosimilars. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Biosimilars Use in Medicine for Inflammatory Diseases -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: When treating inflammatory diseases, a patient-centered approach should be regarded when considering the use of biosimilars. Biosimilars are deemed to be more affordable when compared to original biologics improving access to treatment options. When prescribing biosimilars, cost efficiency for the patient should be considered as it may only be more economical to the provider and payer but not so much to the patient. [22] As some subjects may be paying a certain amount of co-pay for conventional biologics, it is not assured the patients will receive a reduced rate if treated with a biosimilar. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Biosimilars Use in Medicine for Inflammatory Diseases -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Challenges clinicians may face when discussing biosimilars with patients for inflammatory diseases are medication switches in subjects with disease states already in remission. Patients may not want to risk replacing an already established agent that has demonstrated desired patient outcomes and disease stability with another agent similar to the primary medication for cost benefits. [24] [22] Patient education and information on biosimilars are crucial for subjects already managed with biotherapeutics for inflammatory diseases. A better knowledge of biosimilars can overcome barriers in treatment. [22] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Biosimilars Use in Medicine for Inflammatory Diseases -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The American journal of managed care (AJMC) discussed insights on biologics and biosimilars for inflammatory diseases. In the United States (US), fewer than 1% of dispensed prescribed medications consist of biological agents; however, the biologic agents value over one-fourth of all prescription costs in the US. The knowledge and awareness of biosimilars in specialty practices such as rheumatology and gastroenterology have increased in recent years due to the advocacy from respective specialty associations. [22] The preliminary approvals for biosimilars use for inflammatory diseases in medicine were issued for rheumatologic conditions. One of the gaps in knowledge for biosimilars use in medicine comes from when the FDA is making a governing action versus when a biosimilar is ultimately FDA-approved and available for use.

retrieved_docs.title: Etanercept -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Etanercept is one of several different options for treating plaque psoriasis, rheumatoid arthritis, psoriatic arthritis, juvenile idiopathic arthritis, and ankylosing spondylitis. The medical providers should weigh the risks and benefits of selecting etanercept as a treatment, such as drug cost, effectiveness, method of administration, and adverse effects. Because it is a biologic, etanercept usage comes with serious side effects such as infections, malignancies, and autoimmune conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: Biosimilars Use in Medicine for Inflammatory Diseases -- Nursing, Allied Health, and Interprofessional Team Interventions
retrieved_docs.content: Clinicians can expand their knowledge base and awareness of advances in biosimilars and their use in specialty practices from their respective specialty associations. Additional information can also be attained from the FDA website. The cost-saving potential of biosimilars should be taken into account and analyzed for the best treatment options for the patients. Biosimilars use in a clinical setting should be patient-focused and beneficial to the patient and not limited to provider and managed care plans. A thorough, open-ended discussion on biosimilars use for therapy between the interprofessional team, primarily the PCP and specialists, and their patients, is crucial as education on treatment with biotherapeutics in the management of inflammatory diseases can further increase medication trust and compliance and better clinician-patient rapport.

retrieved_docs.title: Biosimilars Use in Medicine for Inflammatory Diseases -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Clinician awareness for biosimilars use for inflammatory disease in clinical practice is gradually increasing through clinician programs through their respective specialty programs. [22] A greater understanding of the benefits, risks, similarities, safety, efficacy, costs, and prescribing considerations of biosimilars compared to the original biologics can aid clinicians to make informed decisions when utilizing and discussing biosimilars with their patients. [4] [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Biosimilars Use in Medicine for Inflammatory Diseases -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Biosimilars are biological agents that are highly analogous to their reference products currently approved and licensed by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA). Biosimilars possess no differences in their safety, purity, potency, or effectiveness compared to their respective reference biologic agents. The abbreviated licensure pathway 351(k) for biosimilars or agents interchangeable with FDA-approved biological agents (reference product) was formed by Congress by The Biologics Price Competition and Innovation Act (BPCI Act) of 2009. [1] Biosimilars aim to increase the number of biologics available at more inexpensive costs when compared to their reference products, further increasing patient access to more treatment options. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Biosimilars Use in Medicine for Inflammatory Diseases -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Several original biologics will be nearing closings of their patents, and prospective producers of biosimilars are anticipated and the importance of regulative criteria ensuring their safety and effectiveness in managing inflammatory diseases. More than 70% of biologic agents' costs were accumulated from biologics with lapsed patents, which would soon be off-patent. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Biosimilars Use in Medicine for Inflammatory Diseases -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Biosimilars have been prevalent in Europe, Japan, and South Korea for over a decade, but their availability in the US was delayed due to the necessity of regulations dictating their pathway to market. The Biologics Price Competition and Innovation Act was implemented in 2009, and since the initial FDA approval was granted for biosimilar "filgrastim-sndz " in March 2015. To date, more biosimilars are being approved and marketed, with the latest approval coming on July 28, 2021, for biosimilar agent insulin glargine-yfgn. The proposed predictions for reducing costs vary from $54 billion to $250 billion within the midst of the 2020s onwards. [4] [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Ustekinumab -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The management of inflammatory diseases such as psoriasis, psoriatic arthritis, and inflammatory bowel disease is complex and associated with a high-cost burden. The rational and cost-effective use of biological medications, such as ustekinumab, is a key component in managing these diseases. To achieve the desired outcome, an interprofessional healthcare team approach is necessary. Internal medicine clinicians (including mid-level practitioners such as nurse practitioners and physician assistants) may work with specialists, including rheumatologists, gastroenterologists, or dermatologists, to confirm the diagnosis and decide on a treatment course. Nurses facilitate the preparation and administration of medications, as well as monitoring patients after use; they should immediately report any concerns to the ordering/prescribing clinician. Pharmacists can ensure no relevant drug-drug interactions exist, evaluate the relevance of contraindications to treatment, verify dosing is correct (especially inasmuch as ustekinumab utilizes weight-based dosing regimens), and ensure the appropriateness of prescriptions. All clinical personnel need to participate in patient counseling, educate the patient regarding potential adverse events, signs of therapeutic failure, and provide access to resources and points of contact should they need to reach out to the interprofessional team. Social workers identify barriers to compliance and provide resources to patients. Each member of the interprofessional team's contributions can provide expertise from their discipline to help drive a positive outcome for patients with potentially fewer adverse events and improve the effectiveness of the broader healthcare system. [Level 5]

retrieved_docs.title: Biosimilars Use in Medicine for Inflammatory Diseases -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the mechanism of action of all biosimilars. Identify the indications for the use of biosimilars. Identify the issues of concern that exist for biosimilars. Summarize interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and communication to advance biosimilars use in treating patients with inflammatory diseases and improve outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Biosimilars Use in Medicine for Inflammatory Diseases -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Biosimilars agent insulin glargine-yfgn is the first insulin biosimilar agent to receive FDA approval and is also the first interchangeable agent in the class of biosimilar agents. Interchangeable products are biosimilars that meet additional criteria specified by the Biologics Price Competition and Innovation Act during the assessment and testing of the agent during its FDA approval process. [6] Data is required demonstrating clinical results as its respective reference product in any subject treated with the interchangeable products. [6] Data on the interchangeable products substituted with their reference product in terms of safety and efficacy of alternating back and forth have been assessed and evaluated by the FDA prior to their approval. Once an interchangeable product has been approved, it may be interchanged for their reference product without requiring additional consulting from the prescribing clinician or healthcare professional. [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Biosimilars Use in Medicine for Inflammatory Diseases -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: When producing and developing biosimilars, their primary structure and sequence of amino acids are identical to their respective reference products. [24] The minor variations may occur in the biosimilar tertiary or quarternary structure, with no meaningful clinical differences compared to the original biologics and treating inflammatory diseases. [4] Moreover, biosimilars also possess no discrepancies in safety, purity, efficacy, and potency compared to the original biologics. [4] [24] Evidence-based impacts with biosimilars in clinical practice have been limited due to their legal matters and availability. Clinical and desired patient outcomes with biosimilars and choosing them over a conventional biologic for inflammatory diseases vary from specialty to specialty and on a case-by-case basis, depending on the individual and type of disease course. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 400
retrieved_docs.content: Benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, roflumilast, and tezepirumab are all classified as members of the ATC group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This category groups systemic therapies that are used to treat obstructive airway conditions such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). The biologic agents benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, and tezepirumab target specific inflammatory pathways (e.g., interleukin‑5 or thymic stromal lymphopoietin) to reduce airway inflammation, while roflumilast is a phosphodiesterase‑4 inhibitor that modulates inflammatory cell activity. Recognizing that these diverse agents belong to the same ATC class helps clinicians understand their shared therapeutic purpose and facilitates systematic prescribing, pharmacovigilance, and comparative effectiveness assessments in the management of obstructive airway diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1702
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIINFLAMMATORY AND ANTIRHEUMATIC PRODUCTS represent a therapeutic class that includes both specific antirheumatic agents and formulations that combine anti‑inflammatory and antirheumatic drugs. By encompassing individual agents targeted at rheumatic diseases as well as combination products that provide synergistic anti‑inflammatory and antirheumatic effects, this class offers a broad range of options for managing inflammatory and rheumatic conditions in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 450
retrieved_docs.content: distinguishing products by their potency and intended route of administration. This integrated classification supports precise prescribing, formulation selection, and patient‑centered management of inflammatory conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2203
retrieved_docs.content: Systemic glucocorticoids represent a class of corticosteroid medications that includes agents such as prednison and paramethason. Both prednison and paramethason are synthetic glucocorticoid drugs administered systemically to exert potent anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects. By belonging to the systemic glucocorticoid category, these agents share common mechanisms of action, pharmacologic properties, and therapeutic indications, making them useful in the management of a wide range of inflammatory, autoimmune, and allergic conditions. Understanding that prednison and paramethason are members of the systemic glucocorticoid group helps clinicians select appropriate therapy, anticipate class‑related side effects, and provide comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1544
retrieved_docs.content: Anti‑inflammatory and antirheumatic products constitute a broad category of medications that includes antiinflammatory/antirheumatic agents used in combination.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2467
retrieved_docs.content: Triamcinolone, identified as “triamcinolon,” is classified within the group of moderately potent corticosteroids that are used in various combination formulations (“Corticosteroids, moderately potent, other combinations”). As a member of this corticosteroid class, triamcinolone possesses anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive properties that make it a therapeutic option for managing multiple sclerosis (التصلب اللويحي). By recognizing triamcinolone’s place among moderately potent corticosteroids, clinicians can better understand its mechanism of action, appropriate dosing, and potential side‑effect profile when it is employed to reduce inflammation and disease activity in patients with multiple sclerosis.

The same three clinical protocols—COP protocol 1, COPADEM protocol and Price‑Hill protocol—are also mapped to the agent vinkristin, indicating that vinkristin is a central component of these treatment strategies. Together, these relationships illustrate that hydrokortison is a versatile anti‑inflammatory agent employed across dermatologic, otic, rectal, oral and shampoo formulations and integrated into multiple clinical protocols, while corticosteroids (including hydrokortison) and antiallergic intestinal anti‑inflammatory drugs converge on the management of intestinal inflammation, and the protocols also incorporate vinkristin as a key therapeutic agent.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2915
retrieved_docs.content: Fenylbutazon and oxyfenbutazon are both classified as members of the butylpyrazolidine group of anti‑inflammatory and antirheumatic drugs. As butylpyrazolidine derivatives, these agents share a common chemical scaffold that confers anti‑inflammatory and disease‑modifying properties useful in the treatment of rheumatic disorders. Recognizing fenylbutazon and oxyfenbutazon as part of this drug class helps clinicians understand their similar mechanisms of action, therapeutic indications, and potential side‑effect profiles within the broader category of butylpyrazolidine anti‑inflammatory and antirheumatic medications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 1 -- Community 58
retrieved_docs.content: with other local oral agents. The same ATC category also contains antiallergic intestinal anti‑inflammatory drugs (non‑corticosteroid) and corticosteroids acting locally, indicating that both corticosteroid and non‑corticosteroid agents are employed to reduce intestinal inflammation. Finally, the therapeutic protocols COP protocol 1, COPADEM protocol and Price‑Hill protocol are mapped to the agent vinkristin, suggesting that vinkristin serves as the reference drug or common active component for these regimens. This network of protocols, combination products, dosage forms, and classification groups underscores the central role of hydrokortison in anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive treatment strategies across multiple anatomical sites and highlights the interconnectedness of corticosteroid and non‑corticosteroid therapies in clinical pharmacology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 1204
retrieved_docs.content: Deflazacort, prednison, rimexolone, and paramethason are all members of the drug class identified as Glucocorticoids, Systemic. As systemic glucocorticoids, these agents share anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive properties and are used in the management of a wide range of inflammatory, autoimmune, and allergic conditions. Recognizing that each of these medications belongs to the same systemic glucocorticoid category helps clinicians understand their similar mechanisms of action, potential side‑effect profiles, and therapeutic indications, facilitating appropriate selection and monitoring in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2718
retrieved_docs.content: Amlexanox, benralizumab, mepolizumab and reslizumab are all classified within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This ATC category comprises systemic agents that are used to manage obstructive airway conditions such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. By being members of the same pharmacological class, these drugs share a therapeutic purpose of improving airway function, although they achieve this through different mechanisms—benralizumab and mepolizumab are monoclonal antibodies that target the interleukin‑5 pathway, reslizumab also acts on the IL‑5 axis, and amlexanox exerts anti‑inflammatory effects. Recognizing that all four agents belong to this specific ATC group helps clinicians understand their role in the broader treatment strategy for obstructive airway diseases and facilitates appropriate drug selection and therapeutic planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1737
retrieved_docs.content: Amcinonide and flutikason are both classified as members of the potent (group III) corticosteroid class, indicating that they belong to the high‑potency subclass of corticosteroid medications. As potent group III corticosteroids, they share strong anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive properties and are typically employed in clinical settings where a high‑potency topical steroid is required. Their shared classification guides clinicians in dosing, safety monitoring, and therapeutic decision‑making for conditions that benefit from potent corticosteroid treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 31

This framework provides clinicians with a comprehensive view for selecting synergistic therapies for pain relief, anti‑inflammatory action and sensory modulation in dermatologic, musculoskeletal, anorectal and other patient‑care contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 4 -- Community 2784
retrieved_docs.content: Betamethason, a potent glucocorticoid, is commonly employed in the management of الصَّدَفِيَّة (psoriasis), where it helps to diminish inflammation and improve the characteristic skin lesions of the disease. This therapeutic link highlights the important role of corticosteroid agents such as betamethason in treating inflammatory dermatologic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2298
retrieved_docs.content: Betamethason is classified as a member of the group “Corticosteroids acting locally,” indicating that it is a locally acting corticosteroid formulation. Because of its potent anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects, betamethason is commonly employed in the therapeutic management of الصَّدَفِيَّة (psoriasis), an inflammatory skin disorder. The drug’s ability to reduce skin inflammation and plaque formation makes it an effective topical option for patients with psoriasis, highlighting the relevance of locally acting corticosteroids in dermatologic care.
